text:
This documentary collection carries out a comparative analysis of the Soviet occupation of Romania, Hungary and Austria between 1944 and 1949. Using newly-declassified documents from the archives of the three countries as well as the Russian Federation, this volume offers the first comprehensive analysis of the various aspects of Soviet imperial expansion and the processes of Sovietization in East-Central Europe during this period. The volume is structured along five lines of analysis: the evolution of Soviet post-war foreign policy during the early Cold War years; the mechanisms of the economic exploitation of the occupied areas through war reparations and joint stock companies; the administrative and repressive roles of the Red Army and the NKVD; the activities of Soviet intelligence services; and the fate of prisoners of war and repatriation.

Exploring the differences between the Soviet occupation of Eastern Europe during the post-war era, this study on Romania, Hungary and Austria demonstrates how Stalin's tactics for establishing Soviet dominance took the form of unilateral forced political change in the case of Romania and Hungary, while a four-power administration allowed Austrian sovereignty to survive. According to the author, Soviet occupation was more the result of long term geo-strategic and resource-removal considerations (such as the creation of a permanent defensive barrier from the Baltic to the Black seas) than an immediate response to the West's Marshall Plan initiative. Soviet hegemony and the behaviour of other states in Eastern Europe during this period are explored in new detail.
